title: Smoked Haddock and Leek Pie
subtitle: Flaked smoked haddock in a cream sauce with leeks, under a puff pastry lid
excerpt: Smoked haddock has a particular affinity with cream and leeks. This pie uses a simple veloute sauce built from the haddock poaching milk, enriched with cream, and poured over the flaked fish and softened leeks before a puff pastry lid goes on.
sourceNotes: Traditional British fish pie tradition. Jane Grigson, Fish Cookery (1973): smoked haddock in cream sauce. Delia Smith, Complete Cookery Course (1978): fish pie method with puff pastry lid.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The filling can be made and cooled a day ahead. Assemble and bake on the day.

glossaryTerms.definition: One of the five French mother sauces: a pale roux thinned with a light stock (fish, chicken, or veal) and enriched with cream. For a fish pie, the poaching milk from the haddock becomes the liquid, giving the sauce a pronounced smoky flavour.

Haddock cold-smoked over woodchips, resulting in a pale golden flesh. Distinct from the yellow-dyed version, which is artificially coloured. Finnan haddie is the traditional Scottish variety, smoked on the bone.

content.text: Smoked haddock is sold in two forms:

(pale cream-coloured) and artificially dyed yellow haddock. Either works for this pie, but the natural version has a more delicate, complex smoke. Poach it in milk: the milk becomes the base of a cream
that carries the smoked flavour through the whole dish.

items.prepNote: no salt needed; haddock is salty

content.text: Put the haddock skin-down in a wide saucepan. Pour over the

and bring to a gentle simmer. Poach for 8-10 minutes until the fish flakes easily. Remove the haddock, peel away the skin, and flake the flesh into large pieces, discarding any bones. Reserve the poaching milk.
in a saucepan over medium heat. Soften the onion for 8 minutes, then add the leeks and cook for 3 minutes. Stir in
and cook for 2 minutes. Gradually add the reserved poaching milk, stirring constantly. Add the
and simmer for 5 minutes until thickened. Season with
only; the haddock provides the salt. Fold in the flaked fish. Cool the filling completely before assembling.
Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Spoon the cooled filling into a deep pie dish. Roll or unroll the

and lay it over the top, pressing the edges to the rim. Trim off the excess. Cut a steam vent in the centre. Brush with beaten egg. Bake for 35-40 minutes until the pastry is deeply golden and the filling is bubbling at the vent.

rows.symptom: Pastry lid lifts off or sinks into the filling
rows.cause: Filling was still warm when the pastry was laid on, causing steam to detach the lid
rows.fix: Cool the filling completely. Set a pie funnel or upturned egg cup in the centre of the dish to support the pastry.

rows.cause: Smoked haddock releases a lot of salt into the poaching milk
rows.fix: Never add salt to the sauce; the poaching milk carries enough. Taste before seasoning.

rows.symptom: Pastry is golden but sauce is cold in the centre
rows.cause: Filling was refrigerator-cold when it went into the oven
rows.fix: Leave the filled dish at room temperature for 20 minutes before topping and baking.

rows.symptom: Haddock breaks into very fine pieces and disappears into the sauce
rows.fix: Poach just until the fish flakes when pressed; fold gently in one or two turns, not repeatedly.

content.text: Smoked haddock and leek pie sits at the intersection of the Scottish fish pie tradition and the everyday British comfort kitchen. It is a winter and early spring dish, suited to cold evenings and simple serving with nothing more than a green salad.
